text: The surgical team inspects the abdominal cavity for metastases and performs a laparoscopic ultrasound of the retroperitoneum, visualizing the area for any abnormalities.
The surgeon begins dissection at the porta hepatis, proceeding from right to left to safely isolate the interface between the portal vein and the tumor.
The surgical field displays significant fibrosis, likely resulting from previous treatment effects, which is visually evident during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the replaced common hepatic artery and demonstrates its mobilization through further dissection, continuing the procedure with careful manipulation of the artery.
The surgeon incises the inferior border of the pancreas, identifying the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and creating a retropancreatic tunnel.
The surgeon retracts the common hepatic artery laterally, revealing and developing the portal vein to complete the retropancreatic tunnel.
The surgeon transects the pancreas early in the procedure to facilitate the mobilization of the portion adherent to the portal vein and the celiac axis.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the pancreas up to the point where the tumor involves the portal vein, with visible tissue separation at the transection site.
The surgeon controls the splenic vein using a vessel loop and transects it with a surgical stapler, completing the separation of the vein from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates the area where the pancreas adheres to the portal vein through further dissection, clearly delineating the boundary between these structures.
The surgeon isolates a segment of the portal vein and places a bulldog clamp across it, ensuring the vein is not significantly narrowed during the procedure.
The surgeon sharply transects the portal vein and removes it en bloc.
The surgeon repairs the vessel by over-sewing it with a 5-0 Prolene suture, ensuring successful closure of the transected area.
The surgeon continues dissection in the retroperitoneum, removing lymph nodes en bloc from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon retracts the stomach anteriorly to identify and dissect the celiac axis. The pancreas is retracted anteriorly and to the left, revealing the area of celiac involvement. The celiac axis is further dissected using this approach.
The surgeon dissects the celiac artery down to the level of the aorta, identifying and further dissecting the proximal left gastric artery takeoff.
An ICG angiogram is used to visualize the celiac axis, aiding in the dissection and identification of the main celiac trunk, along with the left gastric and splenic artery takeoffs.
The surgeon transects the celiac axis using a stapler, removing it en bloc just distal to the left gastric artery.
The surgeon mobilizes the remaining retroperitoneal attachments to the pancreas, separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the gastric mobilization by dissecting in both directions, ensuring the stomach is fully freed from its attachments.
The surgeon divides the omental and splenic attachments, fully freeing the specimen from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places the specimen into an endocatch bag and removes it from the abdominal cavity. The pancreatic margin is excised and sent for frozen section analysis.
The surgeon performs an additional retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy, carefully excising lymph nodes located in the aorto-caval groove.
The surgical team takes additional measures to minimize the risk of a post-operative leak after resecting part of the head of the pancreas for an additional margin.
The surgeon over-sews the cut edge of the pancreas with a running proline suture, then applies and polymerizes a layer of Dermabond on top of the suture line.
